questions.answer: B. {(x,y) | y >= x and y in {0,1,2}}
questions.to_find: Reflexive transitive closure of the binary relation S

understand.plain: The relation S contains pairs where y is exactly one more than x, restricted to y in {0,1,2}. So S = {(-1,0),(0,1),(1,2)}. The reflexive transitive closure (R*) first adds all reflexive pairs (z,z) for z in the universe, then closes under transitivity. Since y is restricted to {0,1,2}, the universe contains {0,1,2} plus x-values {-1,0,1}. Adding reflexive pairs (0,0),(1,1),(2,2) and computing all transitive compositions gives exactly all pairs (x,y) where y >= x and y in {0,1,2}.

keywords.explain: The smallest relation containing the original that is both reflexive and transitive. Denoted R* and computed by adding identity pairs and all chains.
If (x,y) and (y,z) are both in a relation, then (x,z) must also be included for transitivity.
keywords.example: (-1,0) and (0,1) in S gives (-1,1) in the transitive closure

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 420 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='420' height='160' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='210' y='28' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Reflexive Transitive Closure of S</text><circle cx='60' cy='100' r='22' class='hi'/><circle cx='160' cy='100' r='22' class='bg'/><circle cx='260' cy='100' r='22' class='bg'/><circle cx='360' cy='100' r='22' class='bg'/><text x='60' y='105' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>-1</text><text x='160' y='105' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>0</text><text x='260' y='105' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>1</text><text x='360' y='105' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>2</text><line x1='82' y1='100' x2='138' y2='100' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2.5' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><line x1='182' y1='100' x2='238' y2='100' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='282' y1='100' x2='338' y2='100' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='210' y='148' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>S original (solid); R* adds reflexive + transitive (all forward pairs)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Directed chain -1 -> 0 -> 1 -> 2; reflexive transitive closure adds self-loops and all forward shortcuts

given.aim: Find the reflexive transitive closure of the relation S = {(-1,0),(0,1),(1,2)}.

terms.meaning: The original binary relation: pairs (x,y) where y=x+1 and y in {0,1,2}
terms.connects: Starting relation whose closure we compute

terms.meaning: Add (z,z) for every element z in the domain/range of the relation
terms.connects: First step toward reflexive transitive closure

terms.meaning: Include (x,z) whenever (x,y) and (y,z) are both in the relation, for any y

given.plan: Enumerate all pairs in S, add reflexive pairs (z,z) for z in {0,1,2}, then apply transitivity to find all pairs (x,y) where y >= x and y in {0,1,2}.

steps.title: List the original relation S
steps.apply: For y=0: x=-1 giving (-1,0). For y=1: x=0 giving (0,1). For y=2: x=1 giving (1,2).
steps.note: Three pairs forming a directed chain: -1 -> 0 -> 1 -> 2

steps.title: Add reflexive pairs (z,z) for z in {0,1,2}
steps.formula_raw: R* includes (z,z) for all z in the universe
steps.apply: Add (0,0),(1,1),(2,2). Note (-1,-1) is excluded because y=-1 is not in {0,1,2}.
steps.note: The universe is restricted by 'y in {0,1,2}'

steps.title: Apply transitivity to complete R*
steps.formula_raw: if (a,b) in R* and (b,c) in R* then (a,c) in R*
steps.note: These are exactly all (x,y) with y >= x and y in {0,1,2}

solution.result: B. {(x,y) | y >= x and y in {0,1,2}}

questions.formula_note: The reflexive transitive closure uses the chain structure of S: transitivity generates all 'reachable' forward pairs, while reflexivity adds identity pairs, yielding y >= x with y in {0,1,2}.

questions.question:
Let R and S be any two equivalence relations on a non-empty set A. Which one of the following statements is TRUE?

A. R union S and R intersect S are both equivalence relations
B. R union S is an equivalence relation
C. R intersect S is an equivalence relation
D. Neither R union S nor R intersect S are equivalence relations
questions.answer: C. R intersect S is an equivalence relation
questions.to_find: Which of R union S, R intersect S is always an equivalence relation

understand.plain: An equivalence relation must be reflexive, symmetric, and transitive. When you intersect two equivalence relations, each of these three properties is preserved because every pair in the intersection must satisfy all three properties in both R and S. However, the union fails transitivity in general: you can have (a,b) in R and (b,c) in S, but (a,c) might be in neither R nor S.

keywords.explain: A relation that is reflexive (every element relates to itself), symmetric (if a relates to b then b relates to a), and transitive (if a relates to b and b to c then a relates to c).
keywords.example: Equality '=' on integers is an equivalence relation

keywords.explain: A pair (a,b) is in R intersect S only if it is in BOTH R and S. All three ER properties pass through intersection.
keywords.example: If aRb and aSb both hold, then a(R intersect S)b

keywords.explain: A pair (a,b) is in R union S if it is in R or S. Transitivity can fail because a chain (a,b) in R and (b,c) in S need not have (a,c) in R or S.
keywords.example: Counterexample: R={same-parity}, S={same-sign} on integers, their union is not transitive

understand.visual_alt: Two overlapping circles representing R and S; their intersection (overlap) is always an equivalence relation while the union (entire area) may fail transitivity

given.aim: Determine which set-theoretic combination of two equivalence relations is guaranteed to remain an equivalence relation.

terms.meaning: First equivalence relation on set A
terms.example: R = {(a,b) | a and b have same parity}

terms.meaning: Second equivalence relation on set A
terms.example: S = {(a,b) | a and b have same absolute value}

terms.meaning: Pairs that are in both R and S simultaneously
terms.example: A pair (a,b) in the intersection satisfies both ER conditions at once

given.plan: Check each of reflexivity, symmetry, and transitivity for both R union S and R intersect S to determine which one always holds all three properties.

steps.title: Verify R intersect S is an equivalence relation
steps.formula_raw: ER requires: reflexive (aRa), symmetric (aRb => bRa), transitive (aRb and bRc => aRc)
steps.apply: Reflexive: since aRa and aSa both hold, a(R inter S)a. Symmetric: if a(R inter S)b then aRb and aSb, so bRa and bSa (by symmetry of R,S), hence b(R inter S)a. Transitive: if a(R inter S)b and b(R inter S)c then aRb,bRc and aSb,bSc, so aRc and aSc (transitivity of R,S), hence a(R inter S)c.
steps.note: All three properties are preserved under intersection

steps.title: Show R union S can fail transitivity
steps.formula_raw: Transitivity of union requires: if a(R cup S)b and b(R cup S)c then a(R cup S)c
steps.apply: Counterexample: A = {1,2,3}, R = {(1,1),(2,2),(3,3),(1,2),(2,1)}, S = {(1,1),(2,2),(3,3),(2,3),(3,2)}. Then (1,2) in R union S and (2,3) in R union S, but (1,3) is in neither R nor S, so R union S is not transitive.
steps.note: The union mixes chains from different ERs breaking transitivity

solution.result: C. R intersect S is an equivalence relation

questions.formula_note: The equivalence-relation formula (reflexive + symmetric + transitive) is key: all three properties are closed under intersection of relations, but transitivity can fail under union.

questions.question:
The time complexity of computing the transitive closure of a binary relation on a set of n elements is known to be:

A. O(n)
B. O(n log n)
C. O(n^(3/2))
D. O(n^3)
questions.to_find: Time complexity of computing the transitive closure of a binary relation on n elements

understand.plain: The transitive closure of a relation on n elements can be computed using the Floyd-Warshall algorithm, which runs in O(n^3) time. It iterates over all triples (i, k, j) checking whether a path from i to j exists via intermediate vertex k. No known algorithm does better than O(n^3) for general dense relations using standard RAM models.

keywords.explain: An O(n^3) dynamic programming algorithm that computes all-pairs reachability (transitive closure) by considering each element as a potential intermediate node.
keywords.example: For a 3-node relation, it performs 3^3 = 27 operations

keywords.explain: For a relation R, its transitive closure R+ contains (a,c) whenever there exists a chain a R b_1 R b_2 R ... R c.

understand.visual_alt: Three nested loops over n elements each checking reachability through intermediate nodes giving n^3 operations

given.aim: Determine the known time complexity for computing the transitive closure of a binary relation on n elements.

terms.meaning: Number of elements in the set (nodes in the corresponding directed graph)
The smallest transitive relation containing the given relation; equivalent to all-pairs reachability in a directed graph
terms.example: Represented as an n x n boolean matrix
terms.connects: The structure we need to compute

terms.meaning: Dynamic programming algorithm for all-pairs shortest path / transitive closure in O(n^3)
terms.example: For each k from 1 to n: T[i][j] = T[i][j] OR (T[i][k] AND T[k][j])
terms.connects: Standard algorithm achieving the O(n^3) bound

given.plan: Apply Floyd-Warshall algorithm: three nested loops over n elements each gives O(n^3). This is the known tight bound for general binary relations.

steps.title: Represent the relation as an n x n boolean matrix
steps.apply: Initialize the n x n adjacency matrix of the relation in O(n^2) time.
steps.note: The matrix representation allows direct application of dynamic programming

steps.title: Apply Floyd-Warshall to compute transitive closure
steps.formula_raw: for k = 1 to n: for i = 1 to n: for j = 1 to n: T[i][j] = T[i][j] OR (T[i][k] AND T[k][j])
steps.apply: Three nested loops each running n times gives O(n^3) total operations.
steps.note: This computes all-pairs reachability, which equals the transitive closure

questions.formula_note: Floyd-Warshall's O(n^3) dynamic programming algorithm is the standard method for computing transitive closure; a new formula floyd-warshall-transitive-closure captures the recurrence.

questions.question:
A relation R is defined on ordered pairs of integers as follows: (x,y)R(u,v) if x < u and y > v. Then R is:

A. Neither a Partial Order nor an Equivalence Relation
B. A Partial Order but not a Total Order
C. A total Order
D. An Equivalence Relation
questions.answer: A. Neither a Partial Order nor an Equivalence Relation
questions.to_find: Whether R is a partial order, equivalence relation, total order, or none

understand.plain: The relation R uses strict inequalities: (x,y)R(u,v) requires x strictly less than u AND y strictly greater than v. The critical test is reflexivity: can (x,y)R(x,y)? This would require x < x which is false. Since R is not reflexive, it can be neither a partial order (which requires reflexivity) nor an equivalence relation (which also requires reflexivity). Both types of relations require that every element relates to itself.

keywords.explain: A relation is reflexive if every element relates to itself: (a,a) must be in the relation for all a. Partial orders and equivalence relations both require this.
keywords.example: (x,y)R(x,y) requires x < x which is impossible

keywords.explain: A relation that is reflexive, antisymmetric, and transitive. Fails here due to irreflexivity.
keywords.example: <= on integers is a partial order; < is not (irreflexive)

keywords.explain: A relation using strict inequalities (< or >) is irreflexive and transitive but never reflexive, hence it is neither a partial order nor equivalence relation.
keywords.example: < on integers is a strict order, not a partial order

understand.visual_alt: The relation defines pairs of integers where first coordinate is strictly smaller and second is strictly larger; since an element cannot have its first coordinate strictly less than itself, reflexivity fails

given.aim: Classify the relation R on ordered pairs of integers defined by (x,y)R(u,v) iff x < u and y > v.

terms.meaning: Ordered pairs of integers being related by R
terms.connects: Elements in the domain of R

terms.term: Strict inequalities x < u and y > v
terms.meaning: Both conditions must hold strictly; equality is not allowed
No element relates to itself; (a,a) is never in R
terms.connects: Consequence of strict inequality that disqualifies R from both PO and ER

given.plan: Test reflexivity first: (x,y)R(x,y) would require x < x, which is always false. Since R is irreflexive, it cannot be a partial order or equivalence relation.

steps.title: Test reflexivity of R
steps.formula_raw: Partial order requires: reflexive (aRa for all a), antisymmetric, transitive
steps.apply: (x,y)R(x,y) requires x < x AND y > y. Both conditions are false for all integers. Therefore R is NOT reflexive.
steps.note: The use of strict inequality < immediately makes R irreflexive

steps.title: Conclude: R is neither PO nor ER
steps.formula_raw: Equivalence relation requires: reflexive, symmetric, transitive
steps.apply: Since R fails reflexivity, it cannot be a partial order (needs reflexivity) and cannot be an equivalence relation (needs reflexivity). Both conditions rule out options B, C, D.
steps.note: Note: R is transitive and antisymmetric, making it a strict partial order, but not a partial order (which requires reflexivity)

solution.result: A. Neither a Partial Order nor an Equivalence Relation

questions.formula_note: Both partial-order-axioms and equivalence-relation require reflexivity; since R uses strict inequalities (x < u, y > v), it is irreflexive and thus qualifies as neither.

formulas.name: Floyd-Warshall Transitive Closure
formulas.latex: T[i][j] = T[i][j] \lor (T[i][k] \land T[k][j]) \text{ for all } k
formulas.plain: for k=1 to n: for i=1 to n: for j=1 to n: T[i][j] = T[i][j] OR (T[i][k] AND T[k][j])
formulas.whenToUse: When computing the transitive closure or all-pairs reachability of a relation or directed graph on n elements

terms.means: number of elements/nodes in the set
boolean: is there a path from i to j?
intermediate element being considered as a relay node

formulas.trap: The complexity is O(n^3) in time and O(n^2) in space; do not confuse with O(n^2) which is only for the space
